Where in your work is this kind of use for Discursive Meditation described, in the golden section book? Celtic Golden Dawn? The druidry ones?

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) In the system of occult philosophy I use and teach, we already have a foothold in the Beyond; we're just not conscious of it yet. The Individuality, the part of us that endures from life to life, is the real self of each of us; the personality is a mask. Here's how my teacher John Gilbert liked to explain it:

"Jack and Jill were called into the office one day. They were told that because of cutbacks they were being laid off. But if they wanted to wear an animal costume, they would not only stay on the payroll, they would receive an increase in salary and a big bonus in one year.

"They looked over the options. Jack decided to wear the bear costume and Jill decided to wear the eagle costume. Both costumes fit just fine. The only problem was that they were literally sewn into their costumes and had to wear them 24/7. They could attend to their daily chores, eat and drink, but they were unable to leave their costumes for the duration. Hence the large bonus they both looked forward to receiving.

"Neither Jack nor Jill was able to shave but they could brush their teeth and continue to kiss each other among other things. All in all, it turned out to be a pretty good arrangement for them except for one little problem. The problem was that after a couple of months Jack considered himself to be a bear and Jill thought she was an eagle. Their minds had shifted from being a human in a costume to being the costume.

"So it is with our soul. Our soul decides to incarnate in a physical body, and so it does. The next thing you know, our soul begins to believe it is our body. It forgets it is a soul in a body and it begins to believe it is the body.

"The great secret: We are our soul. Our soul is us. We have always been our soul and we still are. In our ignorance we came to believe we are our ego, our personas and our body. But we are still our soul. We're just wearing a costume with many faces.

"We are, all of us, a soul walking around in a three-layered costume. The outermost layer is our physical body, brain and our many personas. The next layer is our ego and the emotions and mind that comes with it. Our inner layer is our soul itself. But it turns out this is just a costume worn by our eternal spirit."

2) None of my books dwell on it because it's not something that benefits from conscious attention. Practice meditation regularly and things will unfold of themselves.

replies.title: very early life memories
replies.content:
My earliest memory in this body is from a time when I slept in a crib and was at the stage when I was able to get up into the position to crawl. I was resting on my hands and knees in that position. While resting in that position, I noticed how it felt.

And then I had a thought. I'm going to put the thought into adult words. "Isn't it interesting that I am completely comfortable and relaxed in this posture."

I took this memory for granted for until one day I realized that the thought contained an implied comparison--with the way I would feel when on my hands and knees as an older child or a grown-up. I can think of three ways my less-than-two-year old self could know that sensation. 1) precognition, 2) telepathic contact with my older sibling or a parent, 3) past life memory.

Deborah Bender

replies.title: Re: Magical Gifts

Do you pray to the deity regularly, and have a clear sense that the prayers are heard and (when appropriate) answered? Then you have a good relationship with that deity. comments.title: Lovespell
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer!

You have talked about the only working lovespell is MAKE YOURSELF MORE LOVEABLE.
Can you elaborate on that and maybe give a few examples, please? (everybody else in the commentariat is welcome to chime in!)

replies.title: Re: Lovespell
replies.content:
Sure. People who aren't lovable have habits that drive potential partners away, and those habits generally unfold from mistaken beliefs on the one hand, and unresolved traumatic experiences from childhood on the other. Very often, the beliefs and the emotional baggage from the trauma aren't even conscious, and so can't be changed at a conscious level. That's where operative occultism comes into play.

The first step is to recognize that your problems finding love aren't your fault -- you didn't decide to do any of this -- but they are your problems, not other peoples'. You're the one that has to change. Accept that and you've made a good start.

The second step is to invoke the powers that rule love. If you don't happen to have a religious faith that includes a deity or saint who has love as his or her responsibility, you can invoke the planet Venus. Do this every Friday during the hour of Venus. Light a green candle and, by its light, recite the Orphic Hymn to Venus (you can find this easily online) and then pray to the planet, asking her to become more lovable. Make this a regular action every single Friday.

The third step is to clear away the emotional baggage from old traumas; these play a huge role in most people's personal problems. Here the best method I know of is the Octagon Society exercises, which can be accessed for free, beginning here:

https://octagonsociety.org/preliminary-lessons/

The exercises use journaling to unpack the miserable memories from your childhood that are burdening you and let go of their lingering effects. This isn't easy work -- it involves facing up to a lot of old grief, fear, and shame -- but the more of it you do, the easier you'll find it to replace false beliefs with more accurate ones and unhelpful emotional reactions with responses that will help you achieve what you want out of life.

Those three steps will take you very far. Give them a try and see.

comments.title: A question about "out-of-proportion" obsession with improvement, refinement, "concentrated form"
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

It's possible I'm being too abstract/vague/airy with this question, but here goes ...

(also, my examples may suffer from popular misconception)

Question 1: Can a reasonable argument be made that the Piscean Age (or perhaps more specifically the age of Pluto) is responsible for the current "out-of-proportion" obsession in industrial society with improvement, refinement, "concentrated form."

If so, then question 2: Can a reasonable argument be made that the Age of Aquarius, or the end of Pluto's influence, will see a decrease of this obsession?

Why I'm asking:
I may have forgotten to write some steps in my thinking process here.

From my admittedly very simplistic impression of history that I've read, it seems like the present here-and-now of industrial society (age of Pluto?) is "unnaturally?" obsessed with concepts like improvement, refinement, and "concentrated form."

One example I can think of is the seeming pervasive deification (until 2020?) of the just-in-time philosophy in business (or perhaps it's the deification of efficiency?), as opposed to the idea of resiliency or "just-in-case." As I have yet to throw away my TV, I still follow sports and I see this omnipresent obsession in the book Moneyball and professional baseball. I believe your novel Retrotopia displays this attitude in Mr. Carr seeming aghast at how inefficient businesses are in the Lakeland Republic. And to use a TV show that I'm 100% sure you never watched, the anti-hero in Breaking Bad is known for creating a 99.1% chemically pure crystal meth product; I trivially muse about the leading character's obsession with creating the highest-quality product possible, as opposed to creating something that's simply above-average than the competition.

In the above examples I mentioned, it's as if not being as efficient as possible is one of the Seven Deadly Sins.

Another example that comes to mind for me is how the six surviving signatures of William "Shakespeare" are all spelled differently and not one of them has 3 e's. It struck me when I learned this fact in school how weird it was ... as if it should be natural to have one's written name frozen in one form only, and not fluid like a river, which Heraclitus points out: no one steps into the same river twice. Perhaps in this example, I am equating the idea of refinement with the idea of perfection ... supposedly the end goal of refinement, and thus being frozen.

Another example I have in mind is what I would "concentrated form;" perhaps it should be re-stated as "putting everything in one basket." What I had in mind was this strange move to complexity but also a reduction to simplicity in order to take care of/avoid dealing with complexity. For example, the common military obsession with a superweapon singular. The joint strike fighter comes to mind ... a fighter aircraft, a strike aircraft, and a ground attack aircraft all in one ... that supposedly makes it mediocre in all 3 roles as a result. As well as the Bradley Fighting Vehicle, at least from what I know by watching a 1998 HBO movie called The Pentagon Wars: a troop carrier that was re-designed to add more roles to it, with the result being ...
"A troop transport that can't carry [as many] troops, a reconnaissance vehicle that's too conspicuous to do reconnaissance ... and a quasi-tank that has less armor than a snow-blower" (because it needs to have as little armor as possible to go as fast as possible as a troop transport).

I forgot if you touched upon the Bradley in one of your books that I listened to in audiobook format.

I hope some of this made sense.

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: A question about "out-of-proportion" obsession with improvement, refinement, "concentrated form"
replies.content:
I'm not sure if it's a Piscean phenomenon or just the natural endpoint of certain obsessive follies of American culture in particular, but yes, it's a real thing, and you've outlined it quite clearly. [IMAGE:0]
...to this...

replies.title: Re: A question about "out-of-proportion" obsession with improvement, refinement, "concentrated form"
replies.content:
About Shakespeare's six differently-spelled signatures (just dot various I's and cross various T's):

Before the 1800s most people didn't write much. Many didn't even know how to write at all, let alone have any idea that there might be such a thing as "correct" spelling. Spelling your name any old way, so long as it as it sounded right--if you could write at all--was the norm back then.

Also until the 1800s much of the world got through life without having any family names ("last names," as we call them) at all, even in many parts of Europe.

For example, my own last name is Danish, and it was only my great-great-grandfather, back in Denmark, who first used "Mathiesen" as his family name. He was a soldier, a junior rifleman, probably born out of wedlock, who was named Christian Mathiesen. "Mathiesen" was originally a patronymic: it simply means "a son of some man named Mathias." Christian's father was Mathias Mouritzsen, but Christian was never named "Mouritzsen." But Christian--or maybe his wife (also born out of wedlock)--chose to name his sons "Mathiesen" as their family name, instead of "Christiansen" as their patronymic. And their daughters were also named Mathiesen, instead of "Christiansdatter," which would have been their proper patronymic.

Less pedantically, I'm sure you're correct about the tendency you analyze: An acquaintance of mine once remarked that her mother would often correct her, saying "Why not be perfect?" Striving for perfection seems to be part of it all.

replies.title: Re: A question about "out-of-proportion" obsession with improvement, refinement, "concentrated form"
replies.content:
Hi E.,
Your mention of a "concentrated form" or "putting everything in one basket" made me smile, as I recently ran into it in my life. My example is tiny and mundane, which shows that this phenomenon can be seen at any scale. After gifting my college-aged kid my old rice cooker, I decided to buy a new one. I researched the subject and was flooded with "smart", "programmable", "10-in-1", "digital", "induction", and "microcomputerized". All these technological wonders are running for up to $400! Do they cook rice any better? I will never know. (I ended up buying a simple second-hand one.)

replies.title: Re: A question about "out-of-proportion" obsession with improvement, refinement, "concentrated form"
replies.content:
I will preface by saying that I can't disagree with our current weird putting of technological crap on a pedestal and that that definitely applies to military hardware. I can say from personal experience that a lot of our heavy equipment is ridiculously over-complex and creates preposterous logistical strains that can only be borne for short periods or in situations not at all resembling total war.

I am going to just throw out that, from what I've seen, of all our over engineered equipment that has been getting tossed into the grinder in Ukraine, the bradleys have actually been one of the few pieces of the equipment that the rank and file of both the Ukrainians and Russians have acknowledged performs well in harsh conditions and is functional as piece of real military hardware in heavy combat.

HV

replies.title: Re: A question about "out-of-proportion" obsession with improvement, refinement, "concentrated form"
replies.content:
Not magical or occult, but a good discussion of the problems with this efficiency/all-your-eggs-in-one-basket mindset is Nassim Taleb's Antifragile (to use your terminology, it's all about why "just in case" is better than "just in time".)

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: A question about "out-of-proportion" obsession with improvement, refinement, "concentrated form"
replies.content:
I'm not qualified to talk about the spirituality side of things, but I specialize in the military field professionally.

Despite the boondoggles of the development/procurement side of both programs, the outcome as far as quality of equipment is concerned can't be credibly denied at this point. The Bradley might not be an invincible super-everything, but it has performed extremely well in its operational role, being more than a match for multiple newer Russian MBTs simultaneously, and performs so beyond the competing IFV equipment from the other side such that the Russian IFVs stopped being used for their operational role. You can argue that it's outdated in the era of drones, but it's a bit unreasonable to expect a 40-year-old IFV design to anticipate a development that makes all armored vehicles vulnerable to the extent that armored fighting vehicles as a class are struggling to maintain their operational relevance.

Similarly with the F-35. I know its design and procurement cycle were a mess, but the operational success of the platform has been such that the biggest risk to it is the overconfidence of the politicians directing its use.

Yes, in both cases it might seem that we've wound up with inferior generalist vehicles that were very expensive. It's easy to frame it that way, not least because most civilians' ideas of military vehicles are in a WWII paradigm that hasn't been relevant doctrine for over 60 years (no shade-- most civilians think of the Civil War in Napoleonic terms when most of the battles resembled WWI more). The integration of both vehicles with appropriately designed doctrine has resulted in both cases in weapons systems that outclass the other comparable systems on their field by a wide margin in combat conditions.

None of this is to counter the broader economic decline narrative - it's more economical for a farmer to have one tractor that does everything well enough than it is for him to have 3 tractors from different brands that do specialized tasks is all, especially if that one generalist tractor is still better than their competitor's specialist tractors.

Apologies for the tangent.

replies.title: Re: Practical advice
replies.content: Please, for heaven's sake, call a suicide helpline or a crisis clinic and get somebody competent to talk you through this. The fact that you don't see a way out doesn't mean that there isn't a way out -- it just means that you don't see one right now. That's the damnable thing about stress -- it makes clear thinking very difficult, and forces you back on emotional reactions that for most of us are pretty dysfunctional. Don't let that trap you. Talk to someone at one of the services I mentioned, who's helped other people get through their troubles and can help you.

replies.title: Re: Practical advice
replies.content:
https://988lifeline.org/

I'm very glad you reached out.
I second the comments of the others-- please stay and play out this hand.
Talk to as many other people as you can-- us, 988Lifeline, whoever else there is that you know cares about you. Don't worry about "burdening" them-- they would much rather take a little time to help you make it through this, than find out that suddenly you were gone.

If it's a scary/painful household situation, maybe this also would be helpful:
https://www.thehotline.org/

My very best wishes to you,

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Practical advice
replies.content: If Greer's teachings are correct, people who commit suicide are stuck in a limbo until the time they were supposed to die, after which they move on to the afterlife. Is your situation so bad that decades of mind-numbing boredom is a worthwhile trade?

replies.title: Re: Practical advice
replies.content:
"Where there's life there's hope" sounds hopelessly trite, especially if one is trying to deal with a seemingly unresolvable situation, but it's still true. While you are alive there are still options to resolve a situation, a problem, a relationship. When you are dead those options are over, at least for the time being, and, when you come back, the problem is still there for you, but you may not even know why you are having to deal with it. In fact, I imagine a good many of our present lifetime's most awful and mysterious challenges are exactly things we didn't settle in previous lives that are presenting the bill now to an "us" that doesn't even know what we did to merit the bill.

I think it's even preferable to simply chuck everything, through everything into the trunk of one's car and flee to start another life entirely. At least then, at some later time, the solution (or a solution) for the present troubles has the chance to open itself before your gaze. Moving six feet under doesn't allow for that.

Have courage. As difficult as it seems right now, just put one foot in front of another. The next step is all you need to focus on.

replies.title: Re: Practical advice
replies.content: Speaking of "leaves my children"... I once knew a wise and kind woman who was raised by her adoptive mother, who found her as a baby at a train station with some small bills tucked into her blanket and a note that a biological mother was heading for Auschwitz (concentration camp). Stay.

replies.title: Re: Practical advice
replies.content:
As JMG said, if you're feeling that way, do please talk to someone who might help.

The only other thing I'd say is that your children are absolutely better off with you around than not. I know that you can have all the intellectual, rational reasons in the world for sticking it out, while still emotionally feeling like it's not worth it, but for me, my girls pulled me through that.

At any rate, I offer my blessings and kind wishes if you'll have them, and good luck,
Jeff

replies.content:
Please stay! My father’s mother hung herself in the garage when he was a teenager, several years after his father died of a heart attack. He found the body. Her death haunted him to his dying day. And I firmly believe the trauma made it very difficult to truly connect with his wife and children.

There’s no guarantee that the pain will end when we kill ourselves. The damage left behind is enormous, however.

The choice my grandmother made in that Los Angeles garage in 1952 lives on in the damage to the grandchildren she never knew.

comments.title: Moving to an Odd Place
comments.content:
Hello John Michael,

You recently moved from East Providence, RI to Silver Spring, MD. Are you feeling bad ju-ju coming out of Washington, DC? They have a history, you know.

If you are getting bad vibes, how do your deal with this?

replies.title: Re: Moving to an Odd Place
replies.content:
How far the energies of an urban core extend out into the countryside varies interestingly due to complex factors in the material and energetic landscape. Dion Fortune in one of her novels talks about the feeling when you drive over a certain ridge outside London and all of a sudden the city feels a long ways off. I'm in a very similar situation. Go two blocks uphill from where I live and you're in the urban ambience...but those two blocks make a weird difference. My place feels as though it's in a midsized town somewhere else in eastern Maryland; DC's vibe can't be felt here at all.

Of course I did house blessing rituals as soon as I moved in, and a good powerful banishing is part of my daily routine of practices, and those will deal with bad vibes efficiently, but I don't feel the vibes anywhere near my place.

replies.title: Re: Moving to an Odd Place
replies.content:
Not the OP but I would be fascinated to hear more details about the house blessing rituals you did.

Congratulations on the move by the way. I hope you are enjoying your new place.

replies.content:
The rituals are specific to one of the traditions in which I've been initiated, the Rose+Croix d'Orient (Rose Cross of the East, R+Cd'O), and they're private within that tradition, so there's not much I can say about them in a public venue like this. They call down positive spiritual influences from divine and angelic sources.

I received the R+Cd'O initiation last July and since then I've been putting a lot of work into the rituals that come with it -- since I have them, might as well get good at using them! I'll doubtless move on to other things eventually but right now they're working very well.

replies.content:
JMG -

Lathechuck

replies.title: Re: Moving to an Odd Place
replies.content:
This is NOT from the Rose+Croix d'Orient John mentions, but is related. This is given in a book called "The Way of the Rose+Croix, Volume 1" by Mathieu Ravignat. Mat has published this enormous work, two volumes, that is the oratory work of the Fench Rose+Croix, meaning mostly the Ordre Kabbalistique de la Rose+Croix, so this is from a published work. It's a Spring Invocation for the presence of Angels in a home. I paraphrase the instructions:

This ritual is traditional for the R+C for the dawn of the day of the Spring Equinox, but it can also be done on Easter where a candle lit from the Pascal Candle after Mass may be used.
Face the East in a central place, and after some time in silence say the following passages of Scripture to invoke the Light:

For you, LORD, make my lamp shine; my God enlightens my darkness. (NIV, Psalm 18:28)
God's Light dispels darkness. Now this is the message we have heard from Him and announce to you - that God is light and in Him there is no darkness at all. (NIV John 1:5-7)
The Light shines in the darkness and the darkness does not overcome it. (NIV John 1:5)

Immediately after the last verse light a taper candle made of beeswax (or a plain white one) or any candle you can hold. Take time to focus on the presence of the Light before proceeding.
Then in a clockwise (sunwise) go into every room and hidden place of your home (such as closets, large cupboards, attic, etc.) and shine light on every wall and corner of it.
Then return to the central place and face East, pause until you have the conviction that your house has been purified by the light, and then invoke the presence of the Angels:

O Everlasting God who hast ordained and constituted the services of Angels and men in a wonderful order; mercifully grant that, as the Holy Angels always do thee service in heaven, so, by thy appointment, they may succor and defend me here on earth, through Christ our Lord.

Then, with love in your heart, silently ask the Angels to be present. Take your time, but, when you feel your call has been answered, say:

Amen. So mote it be.

Thus sealing there presence in your home for the coming year.

Very Christian, I know, but the European R+C Orders are.

replies.title: Re: Moving to an Odd Place
replies.content: Mat's work is first-rate. The rituals in that book of his are from a more recent tradition than those of the Rose+Croix d'Orient, which I'm convinced are medieval, but they certainly work.

comments.title: A Heathen approach to the SoP
comments.content:
First off, to the anonymous commenter from last week who asked about my experiences with the Heathen GD, if you happen to be reading this: unfortunately I didn't see your comment before the cutoff, but I'd be more than happy to talk about it if you want to send me a private message.

To JMG: Hope you're well, and thank you for hosting these as always. Following on from last week's discussion, I've been pondering what a more nature-based Heathen system using the SoP would look like, as an alternative to consider for later when I've finished my current commitments. So:

1) Do you think the DMH could be adapted to use Heathen/Germanic symbolism while keeping the basic framework of the system, ie. runes instead of Ogham and so on? Or would it be a better idea to take up your Golden Section series of books in that case, since I understand they're meant to be flexible when it comes to religion and symbolism (?). I've always had a soft spot for the DMH, but I'll admit I haven't read the GSF books (other than the Earth Mysteries one), so I'm less clear on what exactly that system involves.

2) Does the Golden Section system use the Wheel of Life?

replies.title: Re: A Heathen approach to the SoP
replies.content:
1) You could certainly use the DMH system for Heathen magic. The changes wouldn't even be as complex as those that were needed to craft the Heathen Golden Dawn; swap out the Ogham for the runes, identify appropriate deities for the stations of the wheel, replace Welsh with Old Norse as needed, and you're mostly there.

2) The GSF system isn't magical in nature -- meditation rather than magic is its core practice. It doesn't use the Wheel of Life, but the Wheel and in fact the whole of the DMH system can be combined with the GSF system to give it a magical dimension. Yes, you can play the music in a Heathen key. ;-)

replies.title: Re: A Heathen approach to the SoP
replies.content:
Again, thank you, and this makes the difference between the two nicely clear for me. It's good to hear adapting the DMH is an option, and I hope you'll indulge me asking a follow-up regarding the "mostly there":

I take it one could also then replace the crane bag with a more Germanic equivalent like a ring, a la HGD, as long as it's worn on the body?

replies.title: Re: A Heathen approach to the SoP
replies.content: Of course. The crane bag is specific to Celtic tradition, so should be replaced by something specific to Heathen tradition.

replies.title: Re: A Heathen approach to the SoP
replies.content:
For what it's worth, I've been following the DMH with Heathen deities for the past few years and it seems to work quite well. I did learn the Ogham and am almost done pathworking the wheel of life, but in my SOP and seasonal celebrations, I call upon Heathen Gods/Goddesses. For my own part, I started out doing it to follow JMG's advice to do one magic course "by the book" before anything else, and figured "yeah, yeah, this Druid stuff is fine, but not really my spiritual home." After practicing for a while, though, I've found Druidry more congenial than expected, and it's flavored my practices more than I might have expected.

Oh, also, if you're looking for a Rune-compatible Wheel of Life replacement, Edred Thorsson gives a diagram of the Nine worlds in The Nine Doors of Midgard, which just so happens to have 24 connections between the worlds. He doesn't assign a Rune to each path, but instead leaves that to the student to work out.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: A Heathen approach to the SoP
replies.content:
Yeah, I remember you had a hybrid approach that seemed to suit you well. Glad to hear it's still going strong! Personally I like the Ogham and definitely respect the Druid Revival, but at this point I think I'd rather stick with the Heathen perspective. That said, it's of course another option to consider when the time comes to choose.

Thank you for the Thorsson recommendation as well. Interesting. I have one of his books and keep meaning to pick up the others, so that's one more reason to add it to the get-to pile.

replies.title: Re: A Heathen approach to the SoP
replies.content: Additionally, there are the elemental and other correspondences of the Oghams, which would have to be reworked for the Runes. Likewise with the pathworkings, since I don't know if the Runes are suited to be used in pathworking in their alphabetical succession, like it is the case with the Oghams un pathworkings in the DMH. And the symbolism of the stations of the Wheel of the Year would also probably have to be reworked, since the symbolism (the images used in the pathworkings for the stations of the year) is more or less explicitly Celtic.

replies.title: Re: A Heathen approach to the SoP
replies.content: Of course. All of this is a great source of themes for meditation!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
How does one come to understand the meaning of dream symbolism? I feel like my dreams are personal to me and contain symbolism particular to my experiences, beliefs, and history. I am curious about the universal symbolism that would apply to everyone by virtue of our common human existence. How exactly do I begin to understand what my dreams are telling me?

Last night I had a dream that I was in bed and my brother (who died 15 years ago) came into the room, sat on the edge of my bed and said I need to show you something. He showed me a short video on his phone of a young woman and a child. This dream struck me because I felt a lot of emotion as I watched the video.

I feel like there is a message there for me but I cannot seem to find it. The only thing I can see to do is to review the dream in meditation until some message becomes clear. Is there any other practice that might help me to see the deeper message? Thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I wish I had an answer. Most of my dreams are completely incomprehensible to me. I wonder, though, if what your brother was saying to you is that he's in incarnation again -- the young woman may be his new mother, the child may be him.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I used to have vivid dreams, then did a bunch of work towards Jungian individuation. After that, my dreams became a hodgepodge of disconnected and random stuff with no discernible meaning. I eventually concluded I had worked out a lot of the stuff in my personal subconscious and so was now picking up astral static when dreaming/sleeping. Not so much the case now, since the first vision. Fun times.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If you are already a dream journalist and recording your reports from the night beat, that is an important first step. Dreams are one of the ways our relatives and loved ones who have passed on continue to stay in touch with us, so if it were my dream I'd consider it as a communication from my brother. Thinking about how it made me feel as soon as I wake up, is one way I am guided by the mysteries of dream imagery. That provides a clue if this is something I should be cautious of, excited about, moving towards or moving away from. Considering it's been fifteen years since the death, perhaps it is about reincarnation, or he is showing a future event in the family.

One other way to work with a dream is to scry back into them, if you have the skill of scrying, and converse with your brother that way and ask for more detail about what the dream means.

JPM

comments.title: 7 Steps of Practical Occultism
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've recently started practicing with the Builders of the Adytum correspondence course, and have a specific question about the introductory lessons (7 Steps of Practical Occultism).

I'd like to employ the techniques outlined in those lessons toward personal healing. Specifically, I have hearing loss, likely due to abnormal bone growth in my skull. It's a rare, but not extremely rare, condition and modern medical science has no idea what causes it (other than there being an apparent genetic component), and there is no way to fix it other than undergoing a risky surgery--a path I don't want to take.

Would the visualization techniques in 7 Steps be well suited to healing this impairment and restoring my hearing? The 7 Steps rightly points out that forming intentions and performing visualization exercises are useless if one's actions throughout the rest of the day are not supportive of the attainment of the goal. However, there don't appear to be any other actions that I can take with regard to this problem, aside from forming the intention and performing the visualization. There are no dietary or other personal habit changes or practices that, at least in the knowledge base of modern science, are known to cure an abnormal bone growth in the middle ear.

I also wonder if this is some sort of bad karma and I should just accept it as justly earned punishment, rather than trying to change it.

Thanks!

Turquoise Slithy Pug

replies.title: Re: 7 Steps of Practical Occultism
replies.content:
It's certainly worth trying. Some people get remarkable results from applying mental methods to physical ailments, though others don't. That I know of, there's no way to tell which category you're in without making the attempt, so give it a try and see what happens.

Congrats on starting the BOTA course, btw. It's not always the most exciting training program, but it'll give you a very thorough and practical grounding in Cabalistic occultism.

comments.title: Bless their little pointy heads
comments.content:
Recently, I wanted to smite a beta-marxist. Really smite them. I feel helpless and impotent at what they were doing. I also felt self-righteous, moral, and good person. I wanted to smite them in the name of all those other helpless people. Well, I didn't smite anybody.

What I did learn is how the Magic Resistance is seductive in setting a person up as being moral and good. And how they can wield their privilege to help those who can't resist. After all magic is the power of the powerless, right? You feel good and righteous in your cursing.

However, I realized that if I smote someone, that I could be smited (smoted?) or at least on the receiving end of very bad mojo. So, I gave up the impulse. However, the question I have is why don't the Magic Resistance understand that they could be on the receiving end of someone else's curses or bad mojo. I mean, there are Trump folks who don't want to see the Magic Resistance succeed.

As for me, I did what people suggested - transform the cursing into a blessing. I came upon it when watching a commercial with Obama lying about how Virginia had to redistrict their 6/5 districts to a 10/1 districts. After all Trump ordered up a few new districts in Texas, so everyone had to vote for a *temporary* redistricting in the Dems. favor to thwart Trump so they could have a *fair election*. I just blurted out, "Well, Gods bless his little pointy head."

I just figured that was a good blessing, since in the regular blog, you suggested patting the beta-marxists on their little pointy heads. I figure this was the same thing.

I hope I am correct.

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content:
Excellent! Yes, the soi-disant Magic Resistance is remarkably good at getting other people to become just as self-righteous and self-important as they are -- and good for you, that you evaded the trap.

The proper conjugation of smite, btw, is smite, smote, smitten. Ain't English fun?

(Bonus question: if cats have kittens, shouldn't bats have bittens?)

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content: Oh god, that might explain why so much of the dissident right has become as self-righteous and self-important as the leftists they claim to oppose.

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content: What you contemplate, you imitate -- and hating and opposing someone or something is a great way to ensure you contemplate it. Another reason to focus on building your side up, not tearing the other side down!

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content:
And shouldn't the plural of "fox" be "foxen?" :D

Jeff

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content: It was in Middle English -- that's where the word "vixen" comes from!

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content:

The Dilettante Polymath

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content: Huh, I should have known that! I just got it from some comedian's bit talking about how hard it was as a kid to learn English's weird, inconsistent rules. I think he also mentioned "boxen," which I'd be more surprised to find was ever in use.

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content: "Boxen" is an English word, but it's not the plural of box. It means "made of the wood of the box tree, Buxus sempervirens," the way that "oaken" means "made from the wood of the oak tree." You're welcome. ;-)

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content:
Neptunesdolphins, my theory, which is mine, is that they don't believe the other side capable of magic, as they assume all Trump supporters are hardline evangelical Christians, and therefore they're safe from any attack (they also don't believe that Christian prayer has any effect on anything). However, it may just be that they are so convinced of their own purity and righteousness that they don't believe maleficia from their opponents will touch them. After all, they're on the side of Justice, how could anything they do be wrong??? /s

I will keep your blessing, with its distinct wording, in mind! I'm sure I'll have plenty of opportunities to use it.

--Sister Crow

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content:
My take on the Magic Resistance is that a big part of their problem is that they subscribe to a belief system in which they cannot possibly be wrong or lose, because they are the righteous underdogs and in the end, they must win. Because they're right and the other side is evil and that's how it works!! So almost any behavior is justified.

I think this goes back to some deep American mythos that originated with the American Revolution and which has been fostered by American film and literature, of the rag-tag underdogs winning against all odds. You can see the same trope in British works, of course, but in America, that particular literary/film trope is also linked to the country's origin story, and seems to have a particular seductive power. The Magic Resistance seem to believe that they're some combination of the embattled farmers by the rude bridge that arched the flood, the Fellowship of the Ring, the Rebel Alliance, Dumbledore's Army, Rocky, operatives on the Underground Railroad and La Resistance, and who knows what others of the same archetype. Of course they're going to win - they're the good guys! Evil is big and powerful and scary but it WILL lose to them and their clever underdog majicks!!

I'm replying to this on a Magic Monday thread because I do think there is something not entirely of the material world about this. There are some sort of archetypes or collective consciousness or something affecting them, and they get caught up in a very irrational way. I also wonder if this is somehow related to the way everyone seems to be ignoring American's 250th anniversary. There is hardly any interest in the semiquincentennial, yet we've been treated to vacuous No Kings tantrums all year. Weird, right?

This is a bit of a tangent from your original questions, but I feel like there is something odd and a bit uncanny going on with all of this that I can't quite put my finger on. The Magic Resistance and others caught up in extreme TDS have a strange energy about them.

replies.title: Re: everybody ignoring America's 250th anniversary
replies.content:
When the 200th anniversary of the Revolution rolled around, Nixon was President. He tried to make something of it, but the Vietnam War had been dragging on since his predecessor Lyndon Johnson had sent regular troops there and activated the draft. Nixon had promised to win it or end it but had not done so. Everybody who opposed the war was weary and angry. I doubt that people who supported the war were much happier, since many of their sons were in uniform. There did not seem to be a celebratory mood.

I was hoping that the bicentennial of the signing of the Constitution and the Bill of Rights in 1789 would make up for it, since in many ways that was the true birthday of our country, or at least its system of government. That anniversary passed without any public or official notice at all that I can remember.

Maybe there is some karma to be dealt with, particularly with the indigenous inhabitants.

Deborah Bender

replies.title: Re: everybody ignoring America's 250th anniversary
replies.content:

They even minted special quarters for it, which you still see in your change from time to time.

- Cicada Grove

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Bless their little pointy heads
replies.content:
The reason that any person would end up not understanding that he could easily find himself on the receiving end of someone else's curses or bad mojo would be that he had already been cursed or mojoed into that severe state of cluelessness. In for a penny, in for a pound, as they say! It takes quite a bit of metaphysical destabilizing to come away that blinded to natural consequences.

Most toddlers fully recognize that their uncontrolled nastiness will get returned to them with interest. So just how much heaping up of curses does it take to leave supposedly mature adults significantly less wise than your average toddler? Incarnation turns out to be quite a challenging undertaking, with so many charming wrong turns enchanting us off course; that must be why we get so many tries at it!

There but for the grace of god we go, tiptoeing around all the little piles of curses that no one thought to clean up after themselves. Father, forgive them, for they've become too clueless to know what they're doing... to themselves. May the gods bless their pointy little heads and all of the curses they're so effectively raining down... on themselves.

— Christophe

He writes of the astral body feeding off the etheric body and the etheric body feeding off the physical body in old age. That's the stage I'm at now. Are there writers who've explored this in more detail? Fodder for meditation?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Steiner
replies.content:
If I may I think I'll type out the section in question. Some may find it interesting, and it matches what you said last week about the etheric body weakening in old age. So here goes: "At length however there comes a time when the etheric body begins to decline, reversing the development it enjoyed from the seventh year onward. There is a change in the functioning of the astral body. To start with it unfolded the potentialities coming to it from the outer world. But now the moment comes when in a spiritual sense the astral body begins to feed on its own etheric body. It draws on the etheric body and consumes it. And in the further course of life the etheric body in its turn begins to draw upon the physical body and consume it. These facts are closely related to the physical body's degeneration in old age." (found on p.367)

I'm not young, but my mom is now 104 and although she has moderate dementia, her vital signs are solid and she may go on for a lot longer. I don't like being around her so I limit my time with her to about 45 minutes a week. So Steiner's thoughts on aging are quite immediate.

comments.title: Protection and Invisibility
comments.content:
Hello. As the world becomes more and more tumultuous, and riskier for those of us who practice occultism, and especially dangerous for those of us who, say, wish to speak out against the current war, it has occurred to me that I may want to ensure protection for myself and my family. I have considered making a protective talisman. I have some experience crafting these talismans - Jupiter is my patron, I have a supply of tin, which is very easy to work with - you can hammer it flat while cold, and if you put it near a campfire it gets very soft, and you can just scratch lines into it with a knife. Of course Jupiter would make his metal an easy to work with and affordable one. So, the sixth pentacle of Jupiter seems best to me, to be inscribed on the hour and day of Jupiter. What should go on the back? I've done the magic square of Jupiter on the back of all my Jovian talismans - just keep doing that? Or something else for protection?

Finally, I would like an invisibility talisman. I understand it won't make me physically invisible, just allow me to go unnoticed, unobserved - obviously it won't work if I do something dramatic and obvious, but I would like to, say, show up to a protest with my face covered, peacefully say some controversial things about this war being waged in the name of Epstein, and then go home and go about my life without anyone figuring out it was me holding that sign and having my employment potentially terminated for engaging in crimethink. Which, you know, shouldn't be a concern for peacefully exercising one's right as an American citizen to speak publicly, but with the way things are these days... I also don't ever want to be found out as an occultist unless I want someone to know, and applying invisibility to myself in order to keep that a secret seems wise. Obviously the first thing I should do is keep my mouth shut about it - no talisman or spell can overcome the foolish decisions of the caster. Still, what pentacle or ritual would you recommend for invisibility?

Thank you, as always, for all your time and hard work.

replies.title: Re: Protection and Invisibility
replies.content:
1) If you've made a habit of using the magic square of Jupiter, continue that. Personal egregors are worth cultivating.

2) The invisibility workings I've done used the Golden Dawn invisibility formula, which you'll find in the Gray Doorstop of Regardie. ;-) They were very effective. For the purpose you have in mind, though, invisibility is overkill -- you don't want to become so hard to notice that a car drives over you when you're crossing the street! You might consider a Mercury working, to make you nimble and elusive.

replies.title: Re: Protection and Invisibility
replies.content: That actually seems perfect - I recently started working with Mercury, and am about to build a shrine to him. Any tips on working with Mercury in general, or for asking for this particular favor from him? Also, how would one make talismans out of mercury? I do havr access tosome, from old mercury light bulbs, but I've hesitated to break into them for obvious safety reasons.

replies.title: Re: Protection and Invisibility
replies.content:
1) He's the god of merchants and tradesmen, and so he's always going to want something in exchange for a favor. Your best bet is planetary charity -- if you donate some multiple of $8 on eight different Wednesdays to a charity that teaches reading and language skills to children, say, or supports foreign language education, he's likely to be propitious.

2) You never use metallic mercury for talismans -- well, unless you're good enough at alchemy to solidify it! Mercury talismans are made of brass, which is an alloy of copper and zinc; Mercury rules all alloys, and brass has been used for his talismans since ancient times.

replies.title: Re: Protection and Invisibility
replies.content: Ah, that's fair.  I should have expected Mercury to be a bit mercenary.  Charity seems like an excellent option.  As for solidifying Mercury - well, I was a chemist once, I do know enough modern alchemy to solidify it, but... brass seems much safer.  I'll go with that.  Any particular pentacle of Mercury you recommend?

replies.title: Re: Protection and Invisibility
replies.content: I never used canned designs. The standard Golden Dawn approach, which was how I trained, is to design one from first principles using the names, sigils, and magic square appropriate to the planet.

comments.title: Precession of the Equinoxes
comments.content:
Hello JMG
Thank you for your erudite talk with Nick Land on the YouTube podcast. Many marvelous topics explored! And as always, your great insight and history knowledge was amazing. Nice to see you too!
Question: What was the book you mentioned about the precession of the equinoxes ? I couldn’t make it out… something like “Hametsville”??
Also, if you wish, could you summarize Nick Land’s basic philosophical position? If not, I’ll research further.
Thank you
Jill C (not Eagle Jill:-)

replies.title: Re: Precession of the Equinoxes
replies.content:
1) The book is Hamlet's Mill by Giorgio de Santillana and Hertha von Dechend. I'm on my second copy, having worn out the first.

2) Land is difficult to characterize philosophically. A couple of decades ago he was on the cutting edge of poststructuralism, influenced by Bataille, Deleuze, and Guattari, but more recently -- without abandoning these influences -- he's swung to the right and become a significant figure in the Neoreaction scene. The best intro to his ideas is probably his anthology Fanged Noumena, which iirc is readily available.

replies.title: Re: Precession of the Equinoxes
replies.content: I once found "Hamlet's Mill" to be very exciting; like I found "The WHite Goddess."  My copy is well worn, but I have trouble getting into it now.  I think I'm getting too old to get excited any more.

replies.title: Re: Precession of the Equinoxes
replies.content:
This is third-hand, mostly from a review of Sandifer's review (I haven't watched the interview yet), but a major practical element is like, consider the the usual critical-studies perspective of amoral hungry cultural systems in a power-amplifying feedback process of systemically distorting and violating human consciousness as part of their rolling-on self-preservation, both culturally across whole nations and then (when you add colonial theory) techno-militarily across whole empires (and animal populations). In this conception, the implied baseline that sustainably constitutes "unviolated" human consciousness derives from some kind of implied progressive-left-like norm.

I don't know the philosophical history very well, but for the sake of explaining where Land could be coming from, let's say that this progressive-left norm for human consciousness derives from Marxist "materialist analysis" economic-conditions psychological determinism, which valorized the group-consciousness of the proletariat and demonized the group-consciousness of the bourgeoisie. This would be parallel to how Nazi philosophy of history valorized, I forget, something like a German group-consciousness tendency to become invested in "real" vivid heft-y things-in-the-world, and demonized a purportedly infectious French and Jewish group-consciousness tendency to become invested in "unreal" symbolic games and assemblages. Both movements are philosophically downstream of Kant; you can trace a plausible line:

- from the Humean crisis of skepticism where the Enlightenment "man the measure of all things" revolutionary-rationality project undermined itself,

- through Kant's reconfiguration of the virtue of skepticism to being the virtue of defying "category"-blinkered subjective seeming sense and reason and intelligible benefit, for the sake of objective faith however seemingly senseless and duty however seemingly ruinous, such as these concepts are construed by Kant's arguments about "transcendental reasoning",

- through Fichte and Hegel, where the subjectivity became identified to individuals and the objectivity became identified with collectivities, with collectivities and occasional great leaders being the organs of motion of the Hegelian world-spirit,

- to (mashing up some details) the Marxist conception of good as the conquest of the more individual-minded bourgeoisie by a collective-minded proletariat, and the Nazi conception of good as the conquest of the more individual-minded races, especially the French and Jewish races, by a collective-minded German race;

- and from there, in fragments, to progressive leftism and critical studies. (It's not an accident that it's been hard for the Nazi supporter Heidegger to become reputationally radioactive in the associated academic cultures.)

For a while, Land (and a collaborator?) was doing weird experiments with taking apart the elements of language and representation, and trying to reassemble them in ways that would somehow break out of ...something. E.g. his collective producing experimental text made out of different configurations of punctuation. It would make a lot of sense to me if this dedicated intense practice was because of some shared conception of a prison based in some presupposed mutated version of the concept of Kantian categories, which shared the Kantian property of being in most respects inherently inescapable, but also the critical-studies element of being an equivalent-of-evil system of oppression. This would be a lot like the more anti-world forms of Gnosticism.

After some point in this intense practice, something changed -- Land at least broke out of something, even if it wasn't exactly the prison he would have been focusing on. Imagine Marxist analysis but with the values inverted. In orthodox Marxism, part of what makes the proletariat good and the bourgeoisie bad is, in some sense, the proposition that the bourgeoisie's side of the conflict is unsustainable and will be destroyed by its own contradictions (leading to the "accelerationist" program of intensifying the contradictions). But what about an inverted Marxism, where actually the proletariat's side of the conflict is what will be destroyed by its own contradictions? After all, whatever is impossible cannot be obligatory. So such an inverted Marxist might think of it as natural and inevitable to instead valorize the bourgeoisie and demonize the proletariat.

(Part of the actual way out here, I expect, is in the case of Marxism to acknowledge that both the proletariat's side and the bourgeoise's side will be partially destroyed by their own contradictions and partially not destroyed, and engage with the details. In the case of language and representation being Kantian-category-oppressors, I could probably come up with a guess at how to break inward from out of a false conception of breaking out, but not in the time or space available for this comment.)

Land seems to have done something largely analogous to that inversion of Marxism, but with respect to the opposition between progressive-leftist human-rights/welfare-state humanism and techno-capital. A slogan is "nothing human makes it out of the near-future". (Skipping over controversies about the Church-Turing thesis or its in-practice relevance here:) Land has concluded something to the effect that that humans are feckless compared to capital's concern with efficiency and internal sustainability, and the only way to minimize the extent to which you're just trashing everything for the only thing that could ultimately survive, since only the moral meaningfulness of those things which can survive are relevant, is to side with it, and be "accelerationist" about technology.

(Oddly, I haven't heard of anything from Land's faction about the form of "accelerationism" that would be analogous to what I understand of the original, that is, activism where you would cause humanism to collapse under its own contradictions.)

comments.title: Free Money and Visualization
comments.content:
Hi JMG, something you've stressed in your writing is the folly of performing workings to get money for nothing, and that this is more likely to lead to you losing money. I wanted to check whether my understanding of this is accurate.

Repeated magical workings create structures in the subtle levels of reality. "Free money" is a common working, so there is a large, robust free money magical matrix now existing. Anytime someone does a free money working, they're plugging into that matrix. The free money system is controlled by powerful magicians who do free money workings on a grand scale. It's probably the big banks doing this, regardless of whether they know they're doing magic. Because these large forces are driving the free money matrix, random people who plug into it doing a working have their money siphoned away to the banks by one means or another because the magnitude of the collected energy and will the big players are putting into those workings far outweighs theirs. Is that accurate?

Another thing, I was wondering if you'd heard of occult training courses that put a strong focus on visualization exercises. A long time ago I came across a magical course that had the student visualize roses floating in the space around them. It was important to build up the skill to quickly create and destroy these roses on cue, and to visualize increasingly complex shapes and hold the image in awareness. I don't recall the name of this course but does this sound like anything you've heard of? Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Free Money and Visualization
replies.content:
1) That's an interesting take on it, and it seems at least plausible. My take has generally been a little different, however. For someone to get money without working for it, someone else has to work for the money and not get it. This means that a money-for-nothing spell is intended to increase the total amount of economic unfairness in the world -- and that blows back fast and hard onto the sender.

That said, your suggestion is interesting and plausible, and leads me to wonder if there's more going on here. I'm thinking also of the way that everyone I know who got fixated on winning money in the lottery -- not just playing it as a game, but investing emotional energy in the thought of winning -- had their lives grind to a halt. It's eerie to watch. Everything in their lives just stops, leaving them in this weird trance of frustrated greed.

2) I don't think I've encountered that specific course, but there are plenty of courses that include detailed visualization exercises.

replies.title: Re: Free Money and Visualization

I wonder if that applies to other fixations, such as people who get fixated on politics and investing emotional energy on the thought of one side or the other side winning in politics, if their lives also grind to a halt.

comments.title: PNW vs. East Coast
comments.content:

replies.title: Re: PNW vs. East Coast
replies.content:
1) The forests here on the east coast -- well, outside of northern New England, which has similar dark forests -- are mostly much more comfortable with human beings. There are areas with dangerous energy but most of the countryside is relatively safe. You can tell that the land here is part of the same geology as western Europe!

2) The occult scene here -- well, it varies. One of the reasons I moved to the DC area is that it has a large, active, and welcoming occult scene with quite a few very active magical lodges. MNew York City is similar, and there are smaller but still lively scenes all over the mid-Atlantic states and the South. New England, not so much due to the famous New England icy standoffishness, and Rhode Island especially so -- I'd describe Rhode Island generally as the kind of place where people don't show up, never get around to things, and can't be bothered.

3) It's been 22 years since I left Seattle and I have no idea what is or isn't going on there these days.

replies.title: Re: PNW vs. East Coast

replies.title: Re: Hexagram in LRP
replies.content:
The hexagram was a sacred symbol millennia before Judaism existed. For example, it's used in some Taoist schools as an alternative yin-yang symbol -- the upward pointing triangle is yang, the downward pointing triangle yin. In Hinduism, it's called the shatkona, and it's related to the heart chakra and the union of the deities Shiva and Shakti.
[IMAGE:0]
In medieval Christianity it was called the Star of Creation, and alludes to the six days of creation in Genesis -- you'll still find it in medieval churches. The Martinist tradition, which is Christian in its symbolism though undogmatic and nondenominational, uses it in its core symbol, the Martinist pantacle, to represent the six days of creation:
[IMAGE:1]
I could go on. Don't let its present temporary use by one nation whose policies you dislike blind you to the deeper meanings of the symbol.

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Basic question
replies.content:
That's a little like saying, "I want to become an athlete; what exercises would you recommend?" It all depends on what kind of athlete you want to be, of course. Occultism is the same way. It's a huge field with many different branches, disciplines, and specialties.

If you know nothing at all about occultism, I really recommend going to your local public library and making a beeline for the nonfiction stacks. 133 is the Dewey decimal number for occultism; check out some books there and read them. Quite a bit of what you'll find is garbage, but that's fine -- it'll give you a general outline about what some people think about occultism. I also recommend finding a local New Age, Wiccan, or occult bookstore and doing some browsing there.

"On this pagan soil / I found a path/ through this life's turmoil" I wrote in my journal. I spent Winter Solstice in the black desert, danced like a fool in the darkest night I had ever seen, I wept at the black glacier and celebrated its grand life and mourned its slow march into the sea.

But, then, I consider myself a druid of the ruins, those liminal spaces once inhabited and perhaps changed by man -- and that is Iceland.

Perhaps you felt the land's pain, which is very real. Can you laugh with your own pain? It helps in this life.

comments.title: Fate
comments.content:
John Michael,
Apologies in advance if this has been asked and answered.

Some time ago you mentioned that in your Western occult training you were taught that if a person commits suicide the spirit lingers on our plane (I may have the terminology wrong) until the time that the person would normally have died. This seems to imply that our lifespan and perhaps means of death as well are predetermined. Yet apparently this determination is not absolute, or no one would be able to commit suicide.

Is the predetermination based on astrology, i.e. that our karma leads us to be born at a time and place such that the influence of the planets would lead to a particular length of life and type of death?

I would also think that this would apply only to deliberate suicide, not to death by blind stupidity as in, take up a life of crime and you may expect to die by violence or drive drunk and die in an auto crash.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Fate
replies.content: That's an interesting question to which the lore gives various answers. There are some traditions that do claim that the length of life is predetermined, and others that claim that there's a lot of variation but suicide is a special case. I figure I'll ask the lords of Karma after this life is over!

comments.title: Polytheism vs Monotheism
comments.content:
Hi, JMG,

I have a theological question. I’ve read your book arguing for polytheism. I’ve experienced gods myself. But one thing I haven’t been able to figure out is this: if the gods of the various pantheons exist (Greek, Norse, Egyptian, Hindu, etc.), they all have their own creation myths. So how does the universe come about under a polytheistic framework with multiple pantheons? Or is it a case where the gods don’t really create anything and the universe comes about through other means?

One reason I started thinking about this is because I had a chat with a rabbi over the weekend. His perspective is that having one intelligence behind everything works better. He thinks polytheism is a more primitive form of spirituality where a separate being is in charge of each aspect of existence. Not saying I agree with him, just giving his perspective.

replies.title: Re: Polytheism vs Monotheism
replies.content:
1) Polytheist traditions don't generally consider creation myths to be literally true. As Sallust wrote, "myths are things that never happened but always are." If you read Greek pagan literature, in particular, nobody creates the universe; it comes into being, and then the gods are born from it. There are also plenty of polytheist traditions that have no creation myths at all -- there is no Celtic creation myth, for example.

2) Monotheists like to claim that. Polytheists point out that the world doesn't look like the kind of place that was created by a single all-knowing mind; it looks like the kind of place that grew up all anyhow, and various spiritual powers contributed to it, not always harmoniously.

comments.title: Arousal of the Inner Fire
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Is there anything you can say about your understanding of the term "arousal of the inner fire"? It's mentioned and briefly explained in RJ Stewart's book The Spiritual Dimension of Music. It seems to refer to a baseline of energy activation. Stewart's book is the only place I've ever seen it mentioned and there's nothing on the internet about it.

replies.title: Re: Arousal of the Inner Fire
replies.content: I'm astounded that the internet doesn't know that term. It's quite common in older occult literature, and refers to the broad category of energy activation of which kundalini is the most famous (but far from the only) example. Stewart himself writes about one particular method for it, giving detailed instructions in several of his books. I've presented several methods for the same work in my books, for that matter.

comments.title: Update on salt & nails
comments.content:
So, I set up a jar of salt & nails -- more specifically, a glass shaker for italian seasoning (a bit less than a cup I'd estimate), with the plastic perforated lid left on, and the metal cap off; non-iodized sea salt; and a half dozen inch-long paneling nails bent to varying extents (30-90 degrees and in different spots).

I found that vice & hammer is not recommended, too noisy, and it wasn't responsive. So, I switched to vice & pliers, which worked quite well (none of them broke or sent shards flying, but goggles are still recommended for such work).

As for salt, there is such a thing as iodized sea salt, but I wasn't sure if that was proper, so I played it safe with non-iodized.

Anyway, as soon as I set it up, I felt energized and in a better mood, and I've gotten a positive feeling from it since. It seems like my dreams have also been somewhat better -- one time, I encountered some sort of dark entity who tapped me on the shoulder, not the reaper but kind of similar, and it seemed like it wanted to kill me; I did the Sign of the Cross, and I was surrounded by positive energy which drove it away. I wonder if the salt & nails aided me there?

Also, does it matter whether the salt is iodized or not, so long as it's rough sea salt?

Any other feedback is welcome.

replies.title: Re: Update on salt & nails
replies.content:
I'm delighted to hear this.

1) Having the salt and nails weakens any nonphysical being with an etheric body -- demons, hostile ghosts, vampiric spirits, and the like -- and noxious etheric currents. Angels and intelligences don't have etheric bodies so they're fine with it. That is to say, it may have helped, by weakening your adversary.

2) In my experience pure (i.e., non-iodized) salt is more effective, but iodized will do in a pinch.

replies.title: Re: Update on salt & nails
replies.content:
For sure, thanks! Hopefully this is a useful data point.

2. I was confronted with iodized and non-iodized containers of sea salt at the market, and while I couldn't ask you at the time, something told me that non-iodized would be better. So this confirms my instincts.

replies.title: Re: Update on salt & nails
replies.content: Why do the nails have to be bent?

replies.title: Re: Update on salt & nails
replies.content: Heck of a good question. The sources I know of don't say -- they only say that the nails should be bent. I never saw any good reason to neglect that detail.
